verses.text: Job asks why God does not set visible times of judgment, when men move boundary-stones, steal flocks, and rob the widow and fatherless. He grieves over unpunished oppression. The cry, 'why does God delay?' rises from every age that watches the wicked thrive.

The poor are driven like wild donkeys to scavenge, soaked by storms and clutching the rock for want of shelter. Job paints their misery vividly. He feels keenly the plight of the helpless whom the powerful crush.

The fatherless are torn from the breast, the poor groan in the city, yet God seems to lay no charge against the oppressor. The injustice looks unanswered. Faith must hold that God sees, though His reckoning tarries.

Murderers, adulterers, and thieves love darkness, for to them 'the morning is even as the shadow of death.' Sin hates the light that would expose it. Those who do evil flee the day, for their deeds cannot bear God's gaze.

Job grants their seeming permanence is unstable; their portion is cursed, and the grave will swallow them, forgotten even by the womb. Their triumph is fleeting after all. The prosperity of the wicked has no lasting root.

Though God for a time upholds the mighty, His eye is on their ways; they are exalted a little, then gone, cut off like all others. Their day is short. The God who seems to wait will surely bring every proud oppressor low.
